passages.text: The concept of "hauntology," a term coined by Jacques Derrida, describes the persistent and unsettling presence of the past within the present, particularly the way in which lost or unrealized futures continue to shape contemporary experience. Playing on the French words for "haunting" (*hantise*) and "ontology" (*ontologie*), the term suggests a mode of being that is defined by what is no longer or not yet. Derrida initially used it to discuss the spectral legacy of Marxism after the "end of history." The promise of a communist future, though seemingly defeated, continues to "haunt" the present, revealing the inadequacies of triumphant liberal capitalism. Cultural critic Mark Fisher later expanded this idea to diagnose a broader cultural condition in the 21st century, which he saw as characterized by a pervasive nostalgia and an inability to imagine a future that is radically different from the past. For Fisher, our culture is haunted by the ghosts of lost futures.

questions.text: What is the central idea of "hauntology" as described in the passage?According to the passage, what is the relationship between Derrida's and Fisher's use of "hauntology"?
choices: A philosophical study of ghosts and supernatural phenomena.
The idea that the past and unrealized futures continue to influence and define the present.
A political theory advocating for the return of communism.
A critique of the philosophical works of Mark Fisher.
The belief that history has come to a definitive end.
Fisher completely rejected Derrida's original concept.
Derrida borrowed the concept from Fisher to analyze Marxism.
Fisher applied Derrida's specific political analysis to a broader cultural diagnosis.
Derrida and Fisher used the term to describe the exact same phenomenon.
Derrida's use was purely linguistic, while Fisher's was purely political.

passages.text: The "Great Vowel Shift" was a massive series of sound changes in the history of the English language that affected the long vowels. It occurred primarily between the 15th and 18th centuries and marks a major transition from Middle English to Modern English. In this systematic shift, all the long vowels were "raised," meaning the tongue was positioned higher in the mouth to pronounce them. The highest long vowels, /iː/ (as in Middle English "meet") and /uː/ (as in "boot"), could not be raised further and instead became diphthongs, eventually evolving into the /aɪ/ (as in modern "bite") and /aʊ/ (as in "out") sounds. ________________________________________. For example, the word "name" was once pronounced with a long "ah" sound, but the shift raised it to the "ay" sound we use today. This historical change is the primary reason for the notorious inconsistencies between English spelling and pronunciation.

questions.text: Which of the following best completes the blank?The passage states that the Great Vowel Shift is the main reason why:
choices: This process had no significant impact on the pronunciation of short vowels
The shift essentially involved a chain reaction where one vowel moved into the articulatory space of another
English spelling was largely standardized before this sound change was complete
The grammar and syntax of English remained largely unaffected by this change
This phonetic transformation was caused by large-scale migration from continental Europe
English has so many words borrowed from French.
English spelling often does not match its pronunciation.
Middle English is easy for modern speakers to understand.
English has a very simple and consistent vowel system.
the grammar of English changed significantly.

passages.text: The "prisoner's dilemma" is a canonical example from game theory that shows why two completely rational individuals might not cooperate, even if it appears that it is in their best interests to do so. In the classic scenario, two accomplices are arrested and held in separate cells, unable to communicate. (A) The prosecutors offer each prisoner the same deal: if you betray the other (defect) and they remain silent, you go free and they get a long sentence. (B) If you both betray each other, you both get a moderate sentence. (C) If you both remain silent (cooperate), you both get a short sentence on a lesser charge. From each individual prisoner's perspective, betraying the other is always the dominant strategy, regardless of what the other does. If the other prisoner stays silent, betrayal is better than staying silent (freedom vs. short sentence). If the other prisoner betrays, betrayal is also better than staying silent (moderate sentence vs. long sentence). The dilemma arises because when both prisoners pursue their individual rational self-interest, they both end up with a worse outcome (a moderate sentence each) than if they had cooperated (a short sentence each).

questions.text: What is the "dilemma" in the prisoner's dilemma?The passage is structured to first __________, and then __________.
choices: The prisoners do not have enough information to make a decision.
The prosecutors are offering a deal that is morally wrong.
The pursuit of individual rationality leads to a collectively irrational and worse outcome.
The prisoners are forced to cooperate against their will.
It is impossible to determine the most rational course of action.
present a solution / describe the problem
describe the setup of the scenario / analyze the logical choices and their paradoxical outcome
criticize game theory / offer an alternative model
analyze a historical event / draw a general theoretical conclusion
define cooperation / explain why defection is always irrational

passages.text: The "affective fallacy" is a term from literary criticism, coined by W. K. Wimsatt and Monroe Beardsley, to describe the error of evaluating a work of art by its emotional effect on the reader. As key figures in the school of New Criticism, they argued that a poem is an autonomous object whose meaning should be derived from its formal properties—its structure, imagery, and wordplay—not from the subjective and variable psychological response it produces. ( ① ) To judge a poem as "good" because it makes one feel sad is, in their view, to commit this fallacy. ( ② ) This perspective insists on a clear separation between the text and its emotional consequences. ( ③ ) It sought to make literary criticism a more objective and rigorous discipline. ( ④ ) Reader-response criticism, which emerged later, argued that the reader's interaction with the text is central to the creation of meaning. ( ⑤ ) This approach aimed to ground interpretation in the text itself, free from the "relativism" of individual feelings.

questions.text: Which sentence in the passage is irrelevant to the explanation of the "affective fallacy" and New Criticism?The New Critics' argument against the "affective fallacy" is based on the belief that:
choices: ①
②
③
④
⑤
a literary text is an autonomous object with objective properties.
the reader's emotional response is the most important aspect of a text.
all readers have the exact same emotional response to a given text.
the author's biography is the key to understanding a text.
literary criticism should be a subjective and impressionistic activity.

passages.text: The "Overton window" is a model in political science that describes the range of ideas the public is willing to consider and accept. This "window" of acceptable policies can shift over time, moving an idea from being unthinkable, to radical, to acceptable, and finally to popular policy. The theory posits that most politicians are limited to supporting policies that fall within the Overton window if they wish to remain electorally viable. Therefore, the most effective way to change politics in the long run is not necessarily to elect different politicians, but to shift the window itself. This is a task often undertaken by social movements, activists, and think tanks. By consistently advocating for a "radical" idea, they can gradually make it seem less extreme, pulling the window in their direction and making their preferred policies seem like a reasonable compromise.

questions.text: According to the passage, what is the most effective long-term strategy for political change?The passage suggests that the role of politicians is largely:
choices: Electing politicians who support radical ideas.
Focusing only on policies that are already popular.
Shifting the range of ideas that are considered acceptable in public discourse.
Preventing social movements from influencing politics.
Maintaining the Overton window in a fixed position.
to introduce radical and unthinkable ideas to the public.
to act as independent agents who shape public opinion.
constrained by the current boundaries of public acceptability.
to ignore public opinion in favor of expert advice.
to engage in ideological battles with activists and think tanks.

passages.text: The "iron cage" is a sociological concept from Max Weber used to describe the increased rationalization and bureaucratization of modern society. Weber argued that while these rational systems are highly efficient, they trap individuals in an impersonal, rule-governed reality that limits human freedom and creativity. (A) Social action becomes increasingly based on efficiency and calculation rather than on traditional values or emotions. (B) The pursuit of wealth, for example, becomes a purely mechanical activity, stripped of the ethical significance it may have once held. (C) This process leads to a profound "disenchantment of the world," where life is devoid of mystery and spiritual meaning. Weber was deeply ambivalent about this development, recognizing the material benefits of rationalization while lamenting its cost to the human spirit.

questions.text: What is the main idea of the passage?The phrase "disenchantment of the world" refers to:
choices: Modern society offers greater individual freedom than any previous era.
The "iron cage" is Weber's term for the dehumanizing and constraining effects of modern rationalization.
Max Weber was a strong advocate for increasing bureaucracy in society.
The primary characteristic of modern life is an increase in emotional and spiritual activity.
Rationalization has had no significant impact on the structure of modern society.
the economic failure of modern capitalism.
a widespread loss of faith in science and technology.
the loss of magical, mystical, and spiritual meaning in the world.
a political movement aimed at overthrowing bureaucracy.
the preservation of traditional values and emotional expression.

passages.text: The "looking-glass self," a concept from sociologist Charles Horton Cooley, describes the process by which our sense of self is derived from our perception of how others see us. It posits that the self is not an autonomous entity but is constructed through social interaction. The process has three main components: first, we imagine our appearance to the other person; second, we imagine their judgment of that appearance; and third, we experience some sort of self-feeling, such as pride or shame, as a result of that perceived judgment. ________________________________________. It is a reflection of the judgments we attribute to others, whether those attributions are accurate or not. This theory highlights the fundamentally social nature of our identity.

questions.text: Which of the following best completes the blank?According to the passage, the "looking-glass self" is formed through:
choices: Our sense of self is therefore an innate and fixed biological trait
This process is entirely independent of our social interactions
Our identity is thus not who we think we are, but who we think others think we are
This demonstrates that we have a perfectly objective and accurate view of ourselves
This theory applies only to children and not to adults
solitary introspection and self-analysis.
a process of social interaction and perceived judgments.
the achievement of economic and professional success.
a rejection of societal norms and values.
a direct and accurate understanding of what others think.

passages.text: The "general adaptation syndrome" (GAS) is a three-stage model describing the physiological response to stress, first identified by Hans Selye. The first stage is the "alarm reaction," where the body recognizes a stressor and activates the fight-or-flight response. ( ① ) This is followed by the second stage, "resistance," where the body adapts to the continued high level of stress. ( ② ) While the outward signs of alarm may disappear, the body remains on high alert, consuming resources at an accelerated rate. ( ③ ) This resistance, however, is not sustainable over the long term. ( ④ ) If the stressor continues beyond the body's capacity, the organism enters the third stage, "exhaustion." ( ⑤ ) In this final stage, the body's resources are depleted, leading to burnout, illness, and in extreme cases, death.

questions.text: Choose the most logical place for the given sentence to be inserted in the passage.This initial shock provides a burst of energy to deal with the perceived threat.What is the main purpose of the GAS model?
choices: ①
②
③
④
⑤
To provide a method for eliminating all stress from life.
To describe a predictable, three-stage physiological pattern of response to prolonged stress.
To argue that stress is a purely psychological phenomenon with no physical effects.
To rank different types of stressors based on their severity.
To prove that the exhaustion stage is the most common response to stress.

passages.text: The "Copenhagen interpretation" of quantum mechanics, formulated by Niels Bohr and Werner Heisenberg, is a set of principles that attempt to explain the strange behavior of the subatomic world. A central tenet is that a quantum system does not have definite properties (like a specific position) before it is measured. Instead, it exists in a "superposition" of all possible states, described by a mathematical wave function. The act of measurement forces the system to randomly "choose" one of its possible states, a process known as the "collapse of the wave function." The interpretation famously rejects questions about what the particle was "doing" before the measurement as meaningless. It insists that physics should only be concerned with the results of measurements, as that is all we can ever know. This approach is often summarized as "Shut up and calculate!"

questions.text: According to the Copenhagen interpretation, what happens when a measurement is made?The Copenhagen interpretation's attitude towards the "reality" of a particle before measurement can be best described as:
choices: The particle's true, pre-existing properties are revealed.
The wave function collapses, and the system settles into one definite state.
The physicist can determine all of the particle's properties simultaneously.
The system enters an even more complex state of superposition.
The measurement has no effect on the quantum system.
certain and deterministic.
agnostic and pragmatic.
richly detailed and descriptive.
completely understood and predictable.
identical to the classical physics view.

passages.text: The "precautionary principle" is a framework for policy-making in the face of scientific uncertainty. It states that if an action or policy has a suspected risk of causing severe and irreversible harm to the public or the environment, the absence of full scientific certainty should not be used as a reason to postpone cost-effective measures to prevent it. In essence, it shifts the burden of proof. Instead of regulators having to prove that a new chemical or technology is harmful, the proponents of the activity must prove that it is safe. This principle prioritizes caution and the avoidance of catastrophic risk over potential economic benefits. It is often invoked in debates surrounding climate change, genetically modified organisms, and the introduction of new chemicals, where the potential consequences are vast and not fully understood.

questions.text: What is the core idea of the precautionary principle?The precautionary principle "shifts the burden of proof" by requiring that:
choices: Action should only be taken when there is absolute scientific proof of harm.
Economic benefits should always be prioritized over environmental concerns.
In the face of uncertain but potentially severe risk, it is better to be safe than sorry.
The burden of proof should always fall on those who oppose a new technology.
Scientific uncertainty makes all forms of regulation impossible.
regulators must prove an activity is harmful before stopping it.
the public must demonstrate the exact nature of the risk.
the proponents of an activity must demonstrate that it is safe.
all scientific uncertainty must be eliminated before a decision is made.
only proven, risk-free technologies are ever allowed.

passages.text: The "impostor syndrome" is a psychological pattern in which an individual doubts their accomplishments and has a persistent, internalized fear of being exposed as a "fraud." Despite external evidence of their competence, such as academic degrees or professional success, those experiencing this syndrome remain convinced that they are undeserving and have fooled everyone. (A) This feeling is not a formal mental disorder but is a common experience, particularly among high-achieving individuals. (B) The cycle often involves over-preparing for a task out of fear of failure, then attributing any success to that extreme effort or to luck, rather than to their own ability. (C) This attribution reinforces the feeling of being an impostor, as the success is not seen as evidence of genuine competence. This pattern can lead to chronic anxiety, stress, and a reluctance to take on new challenges.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most logical order of sentences (A), (B), and (C) to follow the introductory sentences?The passage suggests that a key reason for the persistence of impostor syndrome is that individuals:
choices: lack any external evidence of their success.
attribute their successes to external factors like luck or effort, not internal ability.
are generally less competent than their peers.
enjoy the feeling of being a "fraud."
are unwilling to work hard to achieve their goals.

passages.text: The "Matthew effect," a term coined by sociologist Robert K. Merton, describes the phenomenon of cumulative advantage, where those who are already advantaged tend to gain more, while those who are disadvantaged fall further behind. The name comes from a passage in the Gospel of Matthew about the rich getting richer. In academia, for example, a well-known scientist is more likely to receive credit for a collaborative work than a lesser-known colleague. This recognition, in turn, enhances the famous scientist's reputation and access to future funding and opportunities, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of success. ________________________________________. It reveals how initial inequalities, whether in wealth, status, or recognition, can become amplified over time, not necessarily due to differences in merit, but because of the way social systems are structured to reward early advantage.

questions.text: Which of the following best completes the blank?The "Matthew effect" explains how:
choices: This effect ensures that resources are always distributed fairly
This principle applies across numerous domains, from economics to education
This demonstrates that all individuals start with an equal opportunity for success
The Matthew effect is a process that actively works to reduce social inequality
This phenomenon is only observed in the field of academic science
hard work is always the sole determinant of success.
all individuals eventually reach the same level of achievement.
initial advantages can lead to a cycle of increasing success.
disadvantages can be easily overcome in any social system.
social systems are designed to promote perfect equality.

passages.text: The "Turing test," proposed by Alan Turing, is a test of a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from that of a human. In the standard interpretation, a human judge engages in a natural language conversation with a human and a machine, both of which are concealed. ( ① ) If the judge cannot reliably tell the machine from the human, the machine is said to have passed the test. ( ② ) Turing intended this as a pragmatic replacement for the vague philosophical question, "Can machines think?" ( ③ ) It is a purely behavioral test, concerned only with the ability to mimic human conversation. ( ④ ) It does not test for consciousness, self-awareness, or genuine understanding. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, passing the test is a definitive proof that the machine is a conscious, thinking being.

questions.text: Which sentence in the passage contains a logical contradiction to the rest of the explanation?The primary purpose of the Turing test is to:
choices: ①
②
③
④
⑤
measure a machine's computational speed.
determine if a machine has genuine consciousness.
provide an operational, behavioral definition of machine intelligence.
test the linguistic abilities of human judges.
prove that machines can never think like humans.

passages.text: The "Pollyanna principle," or positivity bias, is the psychological tendency for people to remember pleasant items more accurately and frequently than unpleasant ones. The term originates from a novel about a girl who finds something to be glad about in every situation. Research has shown that, on a subconscious level, the mind tends to focus on the optimistic. For example, analysis of language across cultures reveals a universal tendency to use positive words more often than negative words. In memory recall tasks, people consistently remember more positive life experiences than negative ones. This does not mean people are naively optimistic or ignore all threats. Rather, it suggests a subconscious cognitive mechanism that leans toward the positive, which may have evolutionary benefits for resilience and motivation.

questions.text: What is the main idea of the passage?The passage uses language analysis and memory recall tasks as:
choices: The Pollyanna principle is a dangerous bias that leads to poor decision-making.
Humans have a cognitive tendency to focus on and recall positive information more than negative information.
Language and memory are unreliable and should not be trusted.
The novel *Pollyanna* is a major work of psychological theory.
The negativity bias is a more powerful force than the positivity bias.
counterarguments to the Pollyanna principle.
the primary negative consequences of the bias.
evidence supporting the existence of the positivity bias.
methods for overcoming the Pollyanna principle.
examples of conscious, rational thought processes.

passages.text: The "Fermi paradox" highlights the stark contradiction between the high probability of the existence of extraterrestrial civilizations and the utter lack of evidence for them. The argument is straightforward: our galaxy contains billions of stars, many of which are billions of years older than our sun. It seems statistically likely that intelligent life should have arisen on some of the planets orbiting these stars. If even a tiny fraction of these civilizations developed interstellar travel, the galaxy should have been completely colonized or at least visited by now. Yet, we have found no credible evidence of alien activity—no probes, no signals, no artifacts. ________________________________________. Proposed solutions range from the "rare Earth" hypothesis (life like ours is exceptionally uncommon) to the "great filter" theory (some barrier prevents civilizations from reaching an advanced stage).

questions.text: Which of the following best completes the blank?The Fermi paradox arises from a conflict between:
choices: This confirms that our galaxy is teeming with advanced alien life
The central question of the paradox is, "Where is everybody?"
This proves that interstellar travel is a physical impossibility
The paradox is easily resolved by considering the vastness of space
This problem was first proposed by astronomers in the 21st century
scientific theory and religious belief.
the vastness of space and the slowness of time.
statistical probability and a lack of empirical evidence.
the desire for exploration and the cost of space travel.
the existence of life on Earth and the possibility of life elsewhere.

passages.text: The "correspondence bias," also known as the fundamental attribution error, is the tendency to over-emphasize dispositional explanations for others' behaviors while under-emphasizing situational explanations. In other words, we are more likely to attribute someone's actions to their character ("that's the kind of person they are") rather than to the circumstances they are in. (A) For example, if we see a coworker snap at a client, we are more likely to think they are a rude person than to consider that they might be under immense personal stress. (B) This bias is not as strong when we explain our own negative behavior; in that case, we are more likely to blame the situation. (C) This cognitive shortcut, while often efficient, can lead to significant misunderstandings and unfair judgments in our social interactions.

questions.text: What is the main purpose of the passage?The example in sentence (A) illustrates that the correspondence bias can cause us to:
choices: To argue that situational factors are always more important than dispositional ones.
To explain the correspondence bias, a tendency to attribute others' behavior to character over context.
To provide a guide for making more accurate social judgments.
To criticize people for being overly judgmental of others.
To compare the correspondence bias with the self-serving bias.
be more empathetic towards others.
overlook potential situational reasons for someone's behavior.
accurately assess a person's true character.
blame ourselves for the negative actions of others.
focus more on the context than on the person.

passages.text: The "Baader-Meinhof phenomenon," or frequency illusion, is the cognitive bias that occurs when, after noticing something for the first time, one has a tendency to notice it more often, leading to the belief that it has an increased frequency of occurrence. This illusion is the result of two psychological processes working together. The first is selective attention: once your brain is primed to a new word or concept, it is more likely to pick it out from your environment. The second is confirmation bias: each new sighting is interpreted as further proof of the object's sudden ubiquity. ________________________________________. The frequency of the thing you noticed has not actually changed; only your ability to perceive it has, creating a subjective and often amusing illusion.

questions.text: Which of the following best completes the blank?According to the passage, the Baader-Meinhof phenomenon is caused by a combination of:
choices: This process leads to a perfectly objective understanding of the world
In reality, however, this perception of increased frequency is a trick of the mind
This proves that the object's frequency has genuinely increased
This phenomenon is a sign of a superior memory and intellect
This bias is unrelated to the processes of attention and confirmation
a real increase in frequency and a desire for novelty.
selective attention and confirmation bias.
poor memory and a lack of observation skills.
a logical analysis of statistics and objective data.
social pressure and the desire to conform.

passages.text: The "just-world hypothesis" is the cognitive bias that the world is fair and people get what they deserve. This belief can be a psychological comfort, providing a sense of order and control. However, it often leads to a tendency to blame victims for their misfortune. ( ① ) This is because accepting that bad things can happen to good people randomly is more threatening to one's worldview than assuming the victim must have done something to deserve their fate. ( ② ) For example, someone holding this belief might conclude that a victim of poverty is lazy. ( ③ ) This rationalization protects the believer's sense of a just world. ( ④ ) It does so, however, at the cost of empathy and a clear understanding of systemic injustice. ( ⑤ ) This proves that the world is, in fact, a fundamentally just and fair place.

questions.text: Which sentence in the passage contains a conclusion that is directly contradicted by the critical perspective of the passage?The passage suggests that a major negative consequence of the just-world hypothesis is:
choices: ①
②
③
④
⑤
a decrease in personal motivation.
a tendency to overlook social and systemic causes of suffering.
an increase in feelings of anxiety and chaos.
a difficulty in making moral judgments.
a rejection of the idea that good actions should be rewarded.

passages.text: The "backfire effect" is a cognitive bias that causes people to strengthen their beliefs when they are presented with contradictory evidence. When a deeply held conviction is challenged by facts, it can feel like a personal attack, triggering a defensive emotional reaction. (A) Instead of updating their beliefs, people may double down, actively seeking out flaws in the new information and arguments to support their original stance. (B) As a result, the attempt to correct misinformation can end up making the person even more certain that they were right all along. (C) This phenomenon is a significant barrier to productive public discourse and suggests that simply "fact-checking" people is often an ineffective, and sometimes counterproductive, strategy for persuasion.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most logical order of sentences (A), (B), and (C) to follow the introductory sentences?The "backfire effect" implies that:
choices: people easily change their minds when presented with facts.
challenging someone's beliefs can have the opposite of the intended effect.
emotional reactions play no role in how we process information.
misinformation is easy to correct through public debate.
most people are open to considering alternative viewpoints.

passages.text: The "placebo effect" is the phenomenon in which a patient experiences real therapeutic benefits from an inert substance or sham treatment. This effect is not "all in the mind"; it can trigger measurable physiological changes. The patient's belief in the treatment is a key component. The context of the treatment, such as the perceived authority of the doctor and the ritual of administration, also plays a crucial role. Because of this powerful effect, new drugs must be tested in double-blind trials against a placebo to prove their specific pharmacological efficacy. ________________________________________. It demonstrates that our minds can have a powerful, direct influence on our physical health.

questions.text: Which of the following best completes the blank?The passage states that clinical trials use a placebo control group in order to:
choices: This proves that most modern medicines are ineffective
The placebo effect is therefore a major obstacle that modern medicine seeks to eliminate entirely
This highlights the profound and often underestimated connection between mind and body
This shows that a patient's belief has no real impact on their recovery
The effect is a rare phenomenon observed only in a few specific illnesses
enhance the healing power of the patient's belief.
prove that the placebo effect does not exist.
make the new drug more effective.
distinguish the drug's specific effects from the placebo effect.
reduce the overall cost of medical research.
